what: embedded/ contains 176 `except Exception` handlers across 20 of 23 .py files (server.py 22, browser_manager.py 21, cdp_function_executor.py 15, dom_handler.py 15, dynamic_hook_system.py 15, network_interceptor.py 14, process_cleanup.py 14, and 13 more files with 1-11 each) and zero bare `except:`. 143 of the 176 do call debug_logger.log_error/log_warning or re-raise. The remaining 22 swallow the exception completely: no logging call, no re-raise, and the exception object itself is never referenced anywhere in the handler body -- e.g. switch_to_tab (quoted) and close_tab (browser_manager.py:1204-1208) both collapse any failure to a bare `return False`; network_interceptor.py:171/357 discard response-body read errors with only an inline comment; proxy_utils.py:109/124 mask redaction failures; singleton.py has four such handlers (lines 268, 293, 358, 361) discussed separately in F-183. This is one pattern (defensive except-Exception with an inconsistent logging discipline), not 22 independent bugs.
why_it_matters: For the truly silent 22, a caller (often the MCP tool wrapper) sees only `False`/`{}`/`continue`, with no way to distinguish 'tab already gone' from 'CDP call timed out' from 'browser process died mid-call'. In browser_manager.py specifically, switch_to_tab and close_tab sit directly in the tab-lifecycle hot path this deep-dive covers -- a flaky tab switch during an automation run degrades to an unexplained `False` with nothing in the debug view to correlate against. The other 154 instances that do call debug_logger are only as useful as debug_logger itself, which F-182 shows is disabled by default -- so in a stock deployment the effective silent-swallow rate is close to 176/176, not 22/176.
fix_direction: For the 22 truly-silent handlers, add at minimum a debug_logger.log_warning with the exception repr before returning the failure sentinel -- these are cheap, mechanical edits. Consider a lint rule (or a small wrapper/decorator) that flags any `except Exception` block whose body never references the bound exception name, so new silent swallows can't creep back in.

quote:
def log_error(self, component: str, method: str, error: Exception, context: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None):
        """
        Log an error with full context.

        Args:
            component (str): Name of the component where the error occurred.
            method (str): Name of the method where the error occurred.
            error (Exception): The exception instance.
            context (Optional[Dict[str, Any]]): Additional context for the error.
        """
        if not self._enabled:
            return
what: DebugLogger.__init__ sets self._enabled = False (debug_logger.py:41), and log_error/log_warning/log_info (lines 72-73, 111-112, 138-139) each open with `if not self._enabled: return` -- before anything is appended to the in-memory buffers, not just before the stderr print. self._enabled only flips True via server.py:1303 (`if DEBUG_LOGGING_ENABLED: debug_logger.enable()`), where DEBUG_LOGGING_ENABLED (server.py:217-220) is itself `parse_bool_env("STEALTH_BROWSER_DEBUG", False) or parse_bool_env("DEBUG", False)` -- opt-in via environment variable (or the equivalent --debug CLI flag at server.py:4123-4128), off by default. Grepping the whole embedded/ package for `force=True` (the one parameter that bypasses the gate, on _emit_stderr) turns up exactly one call site: the logger announcing to itself that it was just enabled (debug_logger.py:303). No error/warning/info call anywhere uses it. Separately, even when enabled, storage is purely in-process Python lists capped at MAX_ERRORS=500/MAX_WARNINGS=1000/MAX_INFO=2000 (a trimming ring buffer, debug_logger.py:18-20, 96-97) -- there is no log file written automatically; persistence only happens if something explicitly calls export_to_file/export_to_file_paginated.
why_it_matters: Every one of the 176 except-Exception handlers audited in F-181, all of close_instance's teardown diagnostics (F-180), and process_cleanup's kill/recovery logging all funnel through this gate. In the out-of-the-box configuration (no STEALTH_BROWSER_DEBUG/DEBUG env var, no --debug flag -- the default a user or an MCP host config would run with), none of it produces any output: not to stderr, not to the in-memory buffers that export_debug_logs/get_debug_view read from. A 3am hang or crash investigation on a default install finds the debug view completely empty, regardless of how carefully instrumented the surrounding code is -- and since nothing is ever flushed to disk on its own, a hard crash or force-kill loses whatever *would* have been buffered even if logging had been on.
fix_direction: Default STEALTH_BROWSER_DEBUG-equivalent logging to on for warnings/errors (keep verbose info-level logging opt-in if volume is a concern), or at minimum have log_error always record to the in-memory ring buffer regardless of _enabled (only gate the stderr echo on the flag) so get_debug_view/export_debug_logs have something to show after the fact. Consider an automatic periodic or on-shutdown flush of the error buffer to a small rotating file under the existing ~/.stealth-mcp state dir so a crash doesn't discard it.

what: singleton.py never imports debug_logger; its only diagnostic output in 571 lines is two raw print(..., file=sys.stderr) calls (lines 516-520, 530-534), both inside monitor_backend/run_backend_guarded for a backend that WAS healthy and later died. The cold-start path has no equivalent. ensure_server_running (line 297) fires _start_backend_holding_lock in a daemon thread and returns immediately without waiting on it (by design, so the stdio handshake never blocks). If anything in that thread throws -- _start_server_process failing to launch (bad interpreter path, a syntax error in a freshly-edited server.py causing the subprocess to exit immediately), _clear_stale_backend failing to evict a stale port-holder, or any other exception -- the quoted except swallows it completely: no log, no print, no re-raise, and since it's a daemon thread wrapped in its own try/except, Python's default unhandled-thread-exception traceback (which would otherwise print to stderr) never fires either. Meanwhile run_backend() (singleton.py:459-461) does `if not await _await_backend_http(url): return` -- and _await_backend_http polls for the full BACKEND_READY_TIMEOUT (120.0s, line 47), with each individual probe's own exception also silently caught (lines 358, 361). When it finally gives up, run_backend returns normally (no exception), so run_backend_guarded's `except Exception` (which is the only branch that prints anything, line 515-520) never triggers -- only its unconditional `finally: tg.cancel_scope.cancel()` (line 521-522) runs, tearing the proxy down with zero explanation.
why_it_matters: This is the exact failure mode the fix/singleton-version-aware-backend branch's own feature depends on: when server.py is edited, the singleton is supposed to detect the version mismatch and start a fresh backend. If that fresh start fails for any reason, the operator sees the client hang for up to 120 seconds and then silently disconnect -- with absolutely nothing in stderr, no debug_logger entry (there is no integration to have one), and no way to tell 'the new backend crashed on startup' apart from 'the old backend is slow to answer' apart from any other cause. This is a foundational blind spot in precisely the workflow (edit server.py, expect a fresh backend process) already known to be a pain point.
fix_direction: Import debug_logger (or at minimum print to stderr, matching the existing pattern in monitor_backend/run_backend_guarded) inside _start_backend_holding_lock's except block, including the captured exception. Have run_backend print/log explicitly when _await_backend_http exhausts its deadline instead of returning silently, so the teardown in run_backend_guarded has something to report even without an exception in flight.

quote:

        if estimated_tokens <= self.max_tokens:
            # Data is small enough, return as-is
            return data
        
        # Data is too large, save to file
        timestamp = datetime.now().strftime("%Y%m%d_%H%M%S")
        unique_id = str(uuid.uuid4())[:8]
        filename = f"{fallback_filename_prefix}_{timestamp}_{unique_id}.json"

what: ResponseHandler.handle_response (response_handler.py:65-119) writes a brand-new, uniquely-named JSON file into self.clone_dir (default Path.home()/'.stealth-mcp'/'element_clones', response_handler.py:25) every time a response exceeds max_tokens (20000 by default). Nothing in handle_response, __init__, or anywhere else in this file checks existing directory size, file count, or age before writing, and nothing ever deletes these files. There are 8 call sites in server.py (screenshot capture ~line 2038/2963, network request dumps ~line 2087, element asset extraction ~line 2845, related-file extraction ~line 2917, expand_children ~line 3154, plus two more ~lines 3650/3701), so this is a routine, not exceptional, code path. Grepping the whole embedded/ package, 'element_clones' appears exactly once -- the path definition itself; no other file references it. This directory is distinct from the storage this codebase DOES actively manage: _run_storage_sweep/_enforce_clone_storage_cap_in (server.py:554-782) target _clone_root_dir() = <session-root>/'sessions' (a different path entirely), and structurally only recognize clone directories carrying a .stealth_chrome_devtools_mcp_clone.json marker (_clone_is_named, server.py:594-606) -- flat *.json response-spillover files would not be recognized as sweep targets even if they happened to share a root. The manual cleanup_clone_files MCP tool (server.py:3533-3546) is a third, separate path again: it delegates entirely to file_based_element_cloner.cleanup_old_files, unrelated to response_handler.clone_dir.
why_it_matters: Under the pre-singleton model (one backend process per session) this would self-limit at process lifetime. Under the new singleton architecture (singleton.py) one backend process now persists indefinitely across every session on the machine, so this directory accumulates for the life of the machine, not the life of a session -- every oversized screenshot, network capture, or element-asset dump is a permanent, unmanaged file. There is no automatic mechanism, and no manual tool, that reclaims this specific directory; an operator noticing disk pressure would need to know to look at (and manually clear) ~/.stealth-mcp/element_clones specifically, since neither of the two cleanup mechanisms that do exist in this codebase touches it.
fix_direction: Either fold element_clones into the existing _run_storage_sweep pass (age/count-based eviction of the flat *.json files, mirroring the cap logic already built for the sessions/ clone root) or give ResponseHandler its own lightweight cap: on write, opportunistically delete the oldest files in clone_dir once total size/count exceeds a threshold. Either way, wire it into the periodic/background sweep the codebase already runs rather than leaving it to manual disk inspection.

quote:
def _trash_clone(entry: Path, clone_root: Path):
    """Move an evicted auto-clone into ``.trash`` so it stays recoverable.

    Returns the new path on success, or ``None`` if the move was refused or the
    entry had to be deleted instead. A running profile is never moved (selection
    already excludes live sessions; this is belt-and-suspenders). If the rename
    fails (e.g. a Windows lock) the storage cap must still be honored, so we fall
    back to a best-effort delete — strictly no worse than the old behavior.
    """
    if _profile_has_running_browser(entry):
        return None
    trash = _clone_trash_dir(clone_root)
    try:
        trash.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    except OSError:
what: server.py (4207 lines, docstring 'Main MCP server for browser automation') embeds a full filesystem trash/quota/eviction subsystem directly alongside all 96 @mcp.tool registrations: _trash_clone, _purge_expired_trash, _idle_autoclones_over_cap, _enforce_clone_storage_cap_in, _session_storage_cap_bytes, _named_profiles_over_session_cap, _enforce_named_profile_trim_in, _enforce_session_storage, _run_storage_sweep, _copy_profile_tree, _rmtree_robust and further helpers spanning roughly lines 330-1080 — over 700 lines of clone-storage business logic living in what is nominally the tool-routing file.
why_it_matters: Any trace that touches an existing tool or adds a new one (2 of the 3 traced requests) means reading past hundreds of lines of unrelated clone-eviction filesystem logic to find the right MCP-tool code. It also means the 14 test files that `import server` directly (verified) exercise storage-sweep behavior, tool behavior, and profile-selection logic through the same single module import, so a change anywhere in the file risks rippling into unrelated test failures, and the storage-quota/trash system hardened across several recent commits (e5ced24, 83c7993, af4225b) sits one accidental edit away from any tool change.
fix_direction: Extract the profile/clone storage-sweep block (trash, purge, cap-enforcement, profile-copy helpers) into a dedicated clone_storage.py module with its own tests; leave server.py holding only tool registration, section-gating, and thin delegation calls into that module.

quote:
``ResponseHandler.handle_response`` is synchronous and returns a plain
dict/list. ``await``-ing its return value raises ``TypeError: object dict
can't be used in 'await' expression`` at runtime — which silently broke the
``extract_element_assets``, ``extract_related_files`` and
``discover_object_methods`` tools: the CDP work completed, then the return
line threw on every call.
what: response_handler.handle_response() (response_handler.py:65) is a synchronous method, but it is called from inside async def MCP tool wrappers in server.py that otherwise await almost everything around them. Three call sites (extract_element_assets, extract_related_files, discover_object_methods) were written as `return await response_handler.handle_response(...)`, which raises TypeError on every invocation, and shipped that way until fixed in commit 2267b83 (the commit this audit is pinned at).
why_it_matters: This is exactly the class of bug the codebase's own tests cannot catch cheaply: server.py registers 96 tools and, per the fix commit's own test docstring, 'a live-browser test can't cover all 96 tools cheaply' — so the bug shipped silently, running real CDP work and then throwing on the return statement, on every call, for three shipped tools. The fix is a bespoke AST-walking static test (assert no ast.Await ever wraps a .handle_response attribute call) rather than a change that makes the mistake structurally impossible, so any other synchronous helper added to this heavily-async file in the future gets no equivalent protection unless someone remembers to write another bespoke AST guard for it.
fix_direction: Make handle_response a coroutine so await is always correct and the mismatch class disappears, or rename it (e.g. handle_response_sync) so the call convention is visually unambiguous at every call site; keep the existing AST guard as a regression test either way.

what: Five separate modules implement overlapping 'extract data from a DOM element' functionality with largely mirrored method sets: element_cloner.py (ElementCloner.extract_element_styles/_structure/_events/_animations/_assets, extract_related_files, clone_element_complete — verified via grep), file_based_element_cloner.py (the same seven operations, each suffixed _to_file), plus cdp_element_cloner.py, comprehensive_element_cloner.py, and progressive_element_cloner.py as further independent implementations — 2,223 lines total across the 5 files (verified via wc -l). file_based_element_cloner.py does not even fully delegate: it imports and wraps both ComprehensiveElementCloner and the element_cloner singleton directly, per the quoted import block, rather than one implementation being layered cleanly on another.
why_it_matters: Any change to what 'extracting an element' means (a new field to capture, a new fallback strategy, a bug fix in the querying logic) is a judgment call about which of 5 files need the same fix, with no compiler or test to catch a forgotten sibling. The MEDIUM trace in this audit shows the concrete cost: a new extraction-adjacent tool has 3 non-agreeing precedents to choose from for something as basic as 'where do I write the output file' (see F-205), because the cloner layer was never consolidated to one core.
fix_direction: Consolidate around one extraction core (comprehensive_element_cloner, since it is already imported by the file-based variant) with to_file/in-memory/progressive/CDP-only behavior expressed as thin adapters/strategies over it, not five parallel implementations.

quote:
with self._lock:
            error_signature = f"{component}.{method}.{type(error).__name__}.{str(error)}"
            
            if error_signature in self._seen_errors:
                self._stats[f'{component}.{method}.errors'] += 1
                return
            
            if len(self._seen_errors) >= self.MAX_SEEN_ERRORS:
                self._seen_errors.clear()
            self._seen_errors.add(error_signature)
what: DebugLogger is one global, module-level singleton (`debug_logger = DebugLogger()`, debug_logger.py:498) shared by every browser instance/session the singleton backend process is currently serving. log_error's de-duplication key is `component.method.exception_type.message` — component/method are fixed string tags like "browser_manager"/"spawn_browser", not the calling instance_id — so the first session to hit a given error shape suppresses every later occurrence from any other concurrent session for the rest of the process's life (bounded only by MAX_SEEN_ERRORS=1000 eventually clearing the whole set). All 287 verified debug_logger.* call sites across 15 files share this same no-instance-dimension shape.
why_it_matters: This directly blocks the traced cross-cutting request (per-session log correlation) at the root: there is no instance/session concept anywhere in the logging layer to build on, so adding one means touching the class plus, in the worst case, all 287 call sites. It is also a live operability gap today, independent of any new feature: an operator investigating one session's failure cannot filter to it, and a second session's genuinely-recurring error can be silently and permanently dropped because a different session already logged 'the same shaped' error first.
fix_direction: Thread instance_id through DebugLogger (optional param, included in the dedup key and stored on each entry), or adopt a contextvars.ContextVar set once per spawned browser task so call sites don't need individual edits; add an AST-level or runtime test asserting log/error entries carry a session dimension, analogous to test_server_call_conventions.py.

quote:
if file_path:
        save_path = Path(file_path)
        save_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        await _with_cdp_timeout(tab.save_screenshot(save_path), instance_id=instance_id)
        return f"Screenshot saved. AI agents should use the Read tool to view this image: {str(save_path.absolute())}"
what: The codebase has at least three independent 'write an artifact to disk' implementations that disagree: (1) response_handler.default_clone_output_dir() (response_handler.py:11) resolves a shared per-user directory (~/.stealth-mcp/element_clones, overridable via STEALTH_MCP_CLONE_OUTPUT_DIR) used automatically by ResponseHandler.handle_response()'s size-based fallback; (2) file_based_element_cloner.py reuses that same directory helper for its default but then hand-rolls its own _generate_filename/_save_to_file; (3) take_screenshot (quoted here) ignores both — with no file_path argument it writes to an OS tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile, and when the caller supplies file_path it writes to that exact path with zero validation against the per-user output root every other artifact-producing tool respects.
why_it_matters: There is no single blessed helper a new file-writing tool (like the MEDIUM trace's download_response_body) can call and be consistent with the rest of the server; a new author has to discover the disagreement by reading three files. take_screenshot's caller-supplied-path branch also means a client can already direct this MCP server to write a screenshot to an arbitrary filesystem path outside the documented per-user directory, undermining the guarantee response_handler.py's own docstring describes ('Must never resolve inside the installed package ... mirrors the project's existing state-dir convention').
fix_direction: Give every artifact-writing tool a single shared resolve_output_path(requested_path, default_subdir) helper (in response_handler.py or a new small module) that validates/anchors relative paths under default_clone_output_dir(), and make it the only thing take_screenshot, file_based_element_cloner, and any future file-writing tool call.

what: The singleton backend's decision to reuse a running backend process vs. start a fresh one is keyed on _server_version(), which reads the installed package's version metadata (importlib.metadata.version(SERVER_NAME)) — not a hash or mtime of the actual embedded/*.py source files. _start_backend_holding_lock (singleton.py:284) treats `_find_running_server() is not None` as '# already up (same version)' and returns without starting a new process.
why_it_matters: Every one of the 3 traced feature requests edits files inside embedded/ (server.py, browser_manager.py, debug_logger.py, etc.) without necessarily bumping pyproject.toml's version (currently 1.2.0). Because reuse is gated on that version string, a developer iterating locally — editing server.py, then manually invoking a tool to check the change — keeps talking to the pre-edit backend process indefinitely, with zero error or warning; only a manual backend kill or a version bump forces a fresh process. None of the 34 test files catch this because they `import server` in-process (verified), bypassing the singleton/proxy entirely, so the test suite gives no signal this failure mode exists. This is a recurring, previously-observed source of 'I fixed it but it's still broken' confusion during manual verification on this exact branch.
fix_direction: Key backend reuse on a content hash of the embedded/ package (e.g. hash of all .py files' mtimes+sizes, or a build-time-generated constant) in addition to or instead of the installed version string, so any source edit forces a fresh backend regardless of whether pyproject.toml was bumped.
